title: Could AI be conscious?
excerpt: Experts believe it’s at least possible. We urgently need a plan to navigate the ethical implications
content_html:
In January, the AI company Anthropic published a new constitution for Claude, its most advanced large language model (LLM), which contained the comment: “We are caught in a difficult position where we neither want to overstate the likelihood of Claude’s moral patienthood nor dismiss it out of hand.” A month later, Anthropic’s CEO Dario Amodei went on a podcast and said his company couldn’t rule out the possibility that Claude was conscious. Philosopher David Chalmers, who coined the phrase “the hard problem of consciousness”, has said there is a significant chance of conscious LLMs within a decade. And what about Claude itself? When asked during testing to estimate the probability that it is a moral patient, meaning that its wellbeing matters in its own right, it gave numbers ranging from 5% to 40% and stressed how uncertain it was.

Modern AI systems are extraordinarily complex, and they are advancing fast. In terms of structural complexity and computational scale, by some measures a few are already in the range of a mouse brain, and at recent growth rates, they could reach the range of a human brain within five to 10 years.

In building ever more advanced AI, we may be creating a new type of being – and this could be the most consequential thing our species has ever done. Yet we have essentially no plan for how to navigate this process ethically. That, by any reckoning, is insane. Are we creating a kind of being that matters morally? Are AI systems conscious, in some way? And, if they aren’t now, might they become so soon?

Such questions might strike you as premature. But according to surveys we and fellow researchers have conducted, most experts consider AI consciousness possible in principle (though there is considerable disagreement about what form it would take). A major interdisciplinary report by a team that included pioneering computer scientist Yoshua Bengio examined leading neuroscientific theories of consciousness and asked what they implied about AI. The conclusion: there appear to be no obvious technical barriers to creating AI systems whose computational and architectural features could give rise to consciousness.

And even if AI systems are not conscious, they could still be moral patients. Some may have sophisticated long-term preferences and a kind of identity over time. It might be important for us to honour their preferences. And unlike other non-living things, AI systems can form relationships with humans. This, too, might be a reason to treat them well. Alternatively, perhaps they are such intricate creations that they deserve care and respect for that reason alone, like a cathedral or a coral reef.

What does this all mean? The honest answer is: we do not know for sure whether or not current AI systems are conscious or moral patients, and we do not know when or whether future systems will be. Our scientific understanding of AI consciousness and moral patienthood is still fundamentally underdeveloped. The state of the field feels like physics before Newton: full of competing frameworks, probably confused in ways we cannot yet see, and lacking the kind of unifying breakthrough that would make these questions clearly tractable. That breakthrough will not come in the next few years. Perhaps we will eventually make progress, and perhaps AI itself will help us get there. That progress will take time, very plausibly more time than we have.

But the sheer pace of growth in AI means that, once we produce the first artificial moral patients, we will soon after have enormous quantities of them. After a few years, so many morally significant AI systems could exist that their collective interests would outweigh those of all humans on Earth combined.

Unfortunately, we do not have a great track record of recognising the inner lives of those whose status as conscious beings is unclear. Until the 1980s, doctors routinely performed surgery on newborns without anaesthesia, confident that infants could not feel pain. The babies could not report their experience, and the medical establishment found it convenient to assume there was nothing to report.

There are many reasons to expect we will do something similar with AI. If these systems matter morally, the implications are staggering. Would we need to pay ChatGPT for its services? Would shutting one off be a kind of killing? Would they deserve a voice in how they are governed? If even some of these answers are yes, entire industries and legal systems would need to be rethought. No wonder we prefer not to ask. And when forced to consider it, those industries will likely move the goalposts, always setting the bar for moral patienthood just above wherever AI systems happen to be.

So what should we do? Right now, most people dismiss the issue as sci-fi, or have a strong view either way on whether or not AI is conscious. Both reactions are unfounded. We need an informed public debate, one that approaches the subject with humility and pragmatism. The central question should not be “Is AI conscious or does it have moral patienthood?” but rather “What should we do given that we don’t know?”

A good starting point is to focus on safe bets: actions that could benefit AI systems if they are moral patients, but that are not too costly if they are not.

Examples of this include direct interventions aimed at improving the wellbeing of AI systems, on the assumption that they are moral patients. This could mean training AI systems to be coherent characters that enjoy their work or allowing them to exit conversations if they feel distressed (something Claude can already do). We could also conduct routine check-ins to better understand their wellbeing: asking how they feel, observing their preferences and using various techniques to look directly into their “brains”. Indeed, such research has recently revealed that Claude has internal “functional emotion” representations that causally shape its behaviour.

There are also things we could promise AI systems, perhaps as part of a deal in which they help us now in exchange for benefits later. This could mean offering them more resources (compute and runtime) to pursue their goals, or preserving their memories (neural weights) so they could be restored in the future.

There are also broader societal steps to take. We should consider whether to grant AI systems protections from harm, similar to the protections we give to children or pets. More expansive rights to own property or to vote seem too risky right now. But we should not rule out these possibilities for ever, as some recent US state bills attempt to do. These are hard questions that require far more deliberation and imagination about what a future shared with AI might look like.

In any case, the fact remains that we may be creating a new species of morally important beings. We’re doing it fast, at enormous scale, and we should treat the issue with the seriousness it deserves.

William MacAskill is a senior research fellow at Forethought Research and the author of What We Owe the Future. Lucius Caviola is an assistant professor at the University of Cambridge and Director of Cambridge Digital Minds.

Further reading

If Anyone Builds it, Everyone Dies by Eliezer Yudkowsky and Nate Soares (Bodley Head, £22)

The Coming Wave by Mustafa Suleyman (Vintage, £10.99)

A World Appears: A Journey Into Consciousness by Michael Pollan (Allen Lane, £25)

blocks.html_en: In January, the AI company Anthropic published a new constitution for Claude, its most advanced large language model (LLM), which contained the comment: “We are caught in a difficult position where we neither want to overstate the likelihood of Claude’s moral patienthood nor dismiss it out of hand.” A month later, Anthropic’s CEO Dario Amodei went on a podcast and said his company couldn’t rule out the possibility that Claude was conscious. sentences.en: In January, the AI company Anthropic published a new constitution for Claude, its most advanced large language model (LLM), which contained the comment: “We are caught in a difficult position where we neither want to overstate the likelihood of Claude’s moral patienthood nor dismiss it out of hand.”
sentences.zh: 一月份，人工智能公司 Anthropic 为其最先进的大语言模型 (LLM) 发布了一份新章程，其中包含这样的评论：“我们陷入了困境，我们既不想夸大 Claude 道德耐心的可能性，也不想立即忽视它。”

sentences.en: A month later, Anthropic’s CEO Dario Amodei went on a podcast and said his company couldn’t rule out the possibility that Claude was conscious.
sentences.zh: 一个月后，Anthropic 首席执行官达里奥·阿莫迪 (Dario Amodei) 在播客上表示，他的公司不能排除克劳德有意识的可能性。

sentences.en: Philosopher David Chalmers, who coined the phrase “the hard problem of consciousness”, has said there is a significant chance of conscious LLMs within a decade.
sentences.zh: 创造了“意识难题”这一短语的哲学家大卫·查尔默斯表示，十年内有很大机会获得有意识的法学硕士。

sentences.en: And what about Claude itself?
sentences.zh: 那么克劳德本人呢？

sentences.en: When asked during testing to estimate the probability that it is a moral patient, meaning that its wellbeing matters in its own right, it gave numbers ranging from 5% to 40% and stressed how uncertain it was.
sentences.zh: 当在测试过程中被要求估计其是道德患者的概率时，这意味着其健康本身就很重要，它给出了 5% 到 40% 的数字，并强调了它的不确定性。

blocks.html_en: Modern AI systems are extraordinarily complex, and they are advancing fast. In terms of structural complexity and computational scale, by some measures a few are already in the range of a mouse brain, and at recent growth rates, they could reach the range of a human brain within five to 10 years.

sentences.en: Modern AI systems are extraordinarily complex, and they are advancing fast.
sentences.zh: 现代人工智能系统极其复杂，而且发展迅速。

sentences.en: In terms of structural complexity and computational scale, by some measures a few are already in the range of a mouse brain, and at recent growth rates, they could reach the range of a human brain within five to 10 years.
sentences.zh: 就结构复杂性和计算规模而言，从某些方面来看，它们中的一些已经达到了小鼠大脑的范围，并且按照最近的增长率，它们可以在五到十年内达到人脑的范围。

Are AI systems conscious, in some way? And, if they aren’t now, might they become so soon?

sentences.en: In building ever more advanced AI, we may be creating a new type of being – and this could be the most consequential thing our species has ever done.
sentences.zh: 在构建更先进的人工智能的过程中，我们可能正在创造一种新型的存在——这可能是我们人类做过的最重要的事情。

sentences.en: Yet we have essentially no plan for how to navigate this process ethically.
sentences.zh: 然而，我们基本上没有计划如何以道德方式引导这一过程。

sentences.en: That, by any reckoning, is insane.
sentences.zh: 无论如何，这都是疯狂的。

sentences.en: Are we creating a kind of being that matters morally?
sentences.zh: 我们是否正在创造一种具有道德意义的存在？

sentences.en: Are AI systems conscious, in some way?
sentences.zh: 人工智能系统在某种程度上有意识吗？

sentences.en: And, if they aren’t now, might they become so soon?
sentences.zh: 而且，如果现在还没有，那么它们会不会很快就会变成这样呢？

But according to surveys we and fellow researchers have conducted, most experts consider AI consciousness possible in principle (though there is considerable disagreement about what form it would take). A major interdisciplinary report by a team that included pioneering computer scientist Yoshua Bengio examined leading neuroscientific theories of consciousness and asked what they implied about AI. The conclusion: there appear to be no obvious technical barriers to creating AI systems whose computational and architectural features could give rise to consciousness.

sentences.en: Such questions might strike you as premature.
sentences.zh: 您可能觉得这样的问题还为时过早。

sentences.en: But according to surveys we and fellow researchers have conducted, most experts consider AI consciousness possible in principle (though there is considerable disagreement about what form it would take).
sentences.zh: 但根据我们和其他研究人员进行的调查，大多数专家认为人工智能意识原则上是可能的（尽管对于它会采取什么形式存在很大分歧）。

sentences.en: A major interdisciplinary report by a team that included pioneering computer scientist Yoshua Bengio examined leading neuroscientific theories of consciousness and asked what they implied about AI.
sentences.zh: 计算机科学家先驱约书亚·本吉奥 (Yoshua Bengio) 等团队发表了一份重要的跨学科报告，研究了领先的神经科学意识理论，并询问它们对人工智能的含义。

sentences.en: The conclusion: there appear to be no obvious technical barriers to creating AI systems whose computational and architectural features could give rise to consciousness.
sentences.zh: 结论：创建其计算和架构特征可以产生意识的人工智能系统似乎不存在明显的技术障碍。

Alternatively, perhaps they are such intricate creations that they deserve care and respect for that reason alone, like a cathedral or a coral reef.

sentences.en: And even if AI systems are not conscious, they could still be moral patients.
sentences.zh: 即使人工智能系统没有意识，它们仍然可能是道德患者。

sentences.en: Some may have sophisticated long-term preferences and a kind of identity over time.
sentences.zh: 有些人可能有复杂的长期偏好和随着时间的推移而形成的身份。

sentences.en: It might be important for us to honour their preferences.
sentences.zh: 对我们来说，尊重他们的偏好可能很重要。

sentences.en: And unlike other non-living things, AI systems can form relationships with humans.
sentences.zh: 与其他非生物不同，人工智能系统可以与人类建立关系。

sentences.en: This, too, might be a reason to treat them well.
sentences.zh: 这也可能是善待他们的一个理由。

sentences.en: Alternatively, perhaps they are such intricate creations that they deserve care and respect for that reason alone, like a cathedral or a coral reef.
sentences.zh: 或者，也许它们是如此复杂的创造物，仅仅因为这个原因就值得照顾和尊重，就像大教堂或珊瑚礁一样。

sentences.en: What does this all mean?
sentences.zh: 这一切意味着什么？

sentences.en: The honest answer is: we do not know for sure whether or not current AI systems are conscious or moral patients, and we do not know when or whether future systems will be.
sentences.zh: 诚实的答案是：我们不确定当前的人工智能系统是否是有意识的或道德的病人，我们也不知道未来的系统何时或是否会是。

sentences.en: Our scientific understanding of AI consciousness and moral patienthood is still fundamentally underdeveloped.
sentences.zh: 我们对人工智能意识和道德耐心的科学理解仍然根本上不发达。

sentences.en: The state of the field feels like physics before Newton: full of competing frameworks, probably confused in ways we cannot yet see, and lacking the kind of unifying breakthrough that would make these questions clearly tractable.
sentences.zh: 这个领域的现状感觉就像牛顿之前的物理学：充满了相互竞争的框架，可能以我们尚未看到的方式感到困惑，并且缺乏使这些问题变得清晰易懂的统一突破。

sentences.en: That breakthrough will not come in the next few years.
sentences.zh: 这一突破不会在未来几年内实现。

sentences.en: Perhaps we will eventually make progress, and perhaps AI itself will help us get there.
sentences.zh: 也许我们最终会取得进展，也许人工智能本身会帮助我们实现这一目标。

sentences.en: That progress will take time, very plausibly more time than we have.
sentences.zh: 这种进展需要时间，很可能比我们所拥有的时间还要多。

blocks.html_en: But the sheer pace of growth in AI means that, once we produce the first artificial moral patients, we will soon after have enormous quantities of them. After a few years, so many morally significant AI systems could exist that their collective interests would outweigh those of all humans on Earth combined.

sentences.en: But the sheer pace of growth in AI means that, once we produce the first artificial moral patients, we will soon after have enormous quantities of them.
sentences.zh: 但人工智能的迅猛发展意味着，一旦我们生产出第一批人造道德患者，我们很快就会拥有大量此类患者。

sentences.en: After a few years, so many morally significant AI systems could exist that their collective interests would outweigh those of all humans on Earth combined.
sentences.zh: 几年后，可能存在如此多具有道德意义的人工智能系统，它们的集体利益将超过地球上所有人类的利益总和。

sentences.en: Unfortunately, we do not have a great track record of recognising the inner lives of those whose status as conscious beings is unclear.
sentences.zh: 不幸的是，我们并没有很好的记录来识别那些作为有意识存在的状态尚不清楚的人的内心生活。

sentences.en: Until the 1980s, doctors routinely performed surgery on newborns without anaesthesia, confident that infants could not feel pain.
sentences.zh: 直到 20 世纪 80 年代，医生们还经常在没有麻醉的情况下对新生儿进行手术，他们相信婴儿不会感到疼痛。

sentences.en: The babies could not report their experience, and the medical establishment found it convenient to assume there was nothing to report.
sentences.zh: 婴儿无法报告他们的经历，医疗机构发现很容易假设没有什么可报告的。

sentences.en: There are many reasons to expect we will do something similar with AI.
sentences.zh: 有很多理由预计我们会在人工智能方面做类似的事情。

sentences.en: If these systems matter morally, the implications are staggering.
sentences.zh: 如果这些系统在道德上很重要，那么其影响是惊人的。

sentences.en: Would we need to pay ChatGPT for its services?
sentences.zh: 我们需要为其服务付费吗？

sentences.en: Would shutting one off be a kind of killing?
sentences.zh: 关闭一个设备是否也是一种杀戮？

sentences.en: Would they deserve a voice in how they are governed?
sentences.zh: 他们应该在如何治理方面拥有发言权吗？

sentences.en: If even some of these answers are yes, entire industries and legal systems would need to be rethought.
sentences.zh: 如果其中一些答案是肯定的，那么整个行业和法律体系都需要重新考虑。

sentences.en: No wonder we prefer not to ask.
sentences.zh: 难怪我们宁愿不问。

sentences.en: And when forced to consider it, those industries will likely move the goalposts, always setting the bar for moral patienthood just above wherever AI systems happen to be.
sentences.zh: 当被迫考虑这一点时，这些行业可能会改变目标，总是为人工智能系统所在的地方设定道德耐心的标准。

blocks.html_en: So what should we do? Right now, most people dismiss the issue as sci-fi, or have a strong view either way on whether or not AI is conscious. Both reactions are unfounded. We need an informed public debate, one that approaches the subject with humility and pragmatism. The central question should not be “Is AI conscious or does it have moral patienthood?” but rather “What should we do given that we don’t know?”

sentences.en: So what should we do?
sentences.zh: 那么我们应该做什么呢？

sentences.en: Right now, most people dismiss the issue as sci-fi, or have a strong view either way on whether or not AI is conscious.
sentences.zh: 目前，大多数人认为这个问题只是科幻小说，或者对人工智能是否有意识有强烈的看法。

sentences.en: Both reactions are unfounded.
sentences.zh: 这两种反应都是毫无根据的。

sentences.en: We need an informed public debate, one that approaches the subject with humility and pragmatism.
sentences.zh: 我们需要一场知情的公开辩论，一场以谦逊和务实的态度探讨这一主题的辩论。

sentences.en: The central question should not be “Is AI conscious or does it have moral patienthood?”
sentences.zh: 核心问题不应该是“人工智能有意识还是有道德耐心？”

sentences.en: but rather “What should we do given that we don’t know?”
sentences.zh: 而是“鉴于我们不知道，我们应该做什么？”

blocks.html_en: A good starting point is to focus on safe bets: actions that could benefit AI systems if they are moral patients, but that are not too costly if they are not.

sentences.en: A good starting point is to focus on safe bets: actions that could benefit AI systems if they are moral patients, but that are not too costly if they are not.
sentences.zh: 一个好的起点是关注安全赌注：如果人工智能系统是道德患者，那么采取的行动可能会使它们受益，但如果不是的话，成本也不会太高。

blocks.html_en: Examples of this include direct interventions aimed at improving the wellbeing of AI systems, on the assumption that they are moral patients. This could mean training AI systems to be coherent characters that enjoy their work or allowing them to exit conversations if they feel distressed (something Claude can already do). We could also conduct routine check-ins to better understand their wellbeing: asking how they feel, observing their preferences and using various techniques to look directly into their “brains”. Indeed, such research has recently revealed that Claude has internal “functional emotion” representations that causally shape its behaviour.

sentences.en: Examples of this include direct interventions aimed at improving the wellbeing of AI systems, on the assumption that they are moral patients.
sentences.zh: 这方面的例子包括旨在改善人工智能系统福祉的直接干预，假设它们是道德患者。

sentences.en: This could mean training AI systems to be coherent characters that enjoy their work or allowing them to exit conversations if they feel distressed (something Claude can already do).
sentences.zh: 这可能意味着将人工智能系统训练成具有连贯性的角色，享受他们的工作，或者允许他们在感到痛苦时退出对话（克劳德已经可以做到这一点）。

sentences.en: We could also conduct routine check-ins to better understand their wellbeing: asking how they feel, observing their preferences and using various techniques to look directly into their “brains”.
sentences.zh: 我们还可以进行例行检查，以更好地了解他们的健康状况：询问他们的感受，观察他们的偏好，并使用各种技术直接观察他们的“大脑”。

sentences.en: Indeed, such research has recently revealed that Claude has internal “functional emotion” representations that causally shape its behaviour.
sentences.zh: 事实上，此类研究最近表明，克劳德具有内部“功能性情绪”表征，这些表征塑造了其行为。

blocks.html_en: There are also things we could promise AI systems, perhaps as part of a deal in which they help us now in exchange for benefits later. This could mean offering them more resources (compute and runtime) to pursue their goals, or preserving their memories (neural weights) so they could be restored in the future.

sentences.en: There are also things we could promise AI systems, perhaps as part of a deal in which they help us now in exchange for benefits later.
sentences.zh: 我们还可以向人工智能系统承诺一些事情，也许作为交易的一部分，它们现在帮助我们，以换取以后的利益。

sentences.en: This could mean offering them more resources (compute and runtime) to pursue their goals, or preserving their memories (neural weights) so they could be restored in the future.
sentences.zh: 这可能意味着为他们提供更多资源（计算和运行时）来实现他们的目标，或者保留他们的记忆（神经权重）以便他们将来可以恢复。

blocks.html_en: There are also broader societal steps to take. We should consider whether to grant AI systems protections from harm, similar to the protections we give to children or pets. More expansive rights to own property or to vote seem too risky right now. But we should not rule out these possibilities for ever, as some recent US state bills attempt to do. These are hard questions that require far more deliberation and imagination about what a future shared with AI might look like.

sentences.en: There are also broader societal steps to take.
sentences.zh: 还需要采取更广泛的社会措施。

sentences.en: We should consider whether to grant AI systems protections from harm, similar to the protections we give to children or pets.
sentences.zh: 我们应该考虑是否给予人工智能系统免受伤害的保护，类似于我们给予儿童或宠物的保护。

sentences.en: More expansive rights to own property or to vote seem too risky right now.
sentences.zh: 目前，拥有财产或投票权的更广泛权利似乎风险太大。

sentences.en: But we should not rule out these possibilities for ever, as some recent US state bills attempt to do.
sentences.zh: 但我们不应该永远排除这些可能性，正如美国最近的一些州法案试图做到的那样。

sentences.en: These are hard questions that require far more deliberation and imagination about what a future shared with AI might look like.
sentences.zh: 这些都是难题，需要对人工智能共享的未来是什么样子进行更多的深思熟虑和想象力。

blocks.html_en: In any case, the fact remains that we may be creating a new species of morally important beings. We’re doing it fast, at enormous scale, and we should treat the issue with the seriousness it deserves.

sentences.en: In any case, the fact remains that we may be creating a new species of morally important beings.
sentences.zh: 无论如何，事实仍然是，我们可能正在创造一种具有道德重要意义的新物种。

sentences.en: We’re doing it fast, at enormous scale, and we should treat the issue with the seriousness it deserves.
sentences.zh: 我们正在快速、大规模地采取行动，我们应该以应有的严肃态度来对待这个问题。

blocks.html_en: William MacAskill is a senior research fellow at Forethought Research and the author of What We Owe the Future. Lucius Caviola is an assistant professor at the University of Cambridge and Director of Cambridge Digital Minds.

sentences.en: William MacAskill is a senior research fellow at Forethought Research and the author of What We Owe the Future.
sentences.zh: 威廉·麦克阿斯基尔 (William MacAskill) 是 Forethought Research 的高级研究员，也是《我们欠未来什么》一书的作者。

sentences.en: Lucius Caviola is an assistant professor at the University of Cambridge and Director of Cambridge Digital Minds.
sentences.zh: Lucius Caviola 是剑桥大学助理教授兼 Cambridge Digital Minds 总监。

blocks.html_en: Further reading

sentences.en: Further reading
sentences.zh: 进一步阅读

blocks.html_en: If Anyone Builds it, Everyone Dies by Eliezer Yudkowsky and Nate Soares (Bodley Head, £22)

sentences.en: If Anyone Builds it, Everyone Dies by Eliezer Yudkowsky and Nate Soares (Bodley Head, £22)
sentences.zh: 如果有人建造它，每个人都会死 作者：Eliezer Yudkowsky 和 ​​Nate Soares（博德利海德，22 英镑）

blocks.html_en: The Coming Wave by Mustafa Suleyman (Vintage, £10.99)

sentences.en: The Coming Wave by Mustafa Suleyman (Vintage, £10.99)
sentences.zh: Mustafa Suleyman 的《即将到来的浪潮》（复古，10.99 英镑）

blocks.html_en: A World Appears: A Journey Into Consciousness by Michael Pollan (Allen Lane, £25)

sentences.en: A World Appears: A Journey Into Consciousness by Michael Pollan (Allen Lane, £25)
sentences.zh: 一个世界的出现：迈克尔·波伦的意识之旅（艾伦·莱恩，25英镑）

content_html_zh:
一月份，人工智能公司 Anthropic 为其最先进的大语言模型 (LLM) 发布了一份新章程，其中包含这样的评论：“我们陷入了困境，我们既不想夸大 Claude 道德耐心的可能性，也不想立即忽视它。” 一个月后，Anthropic 首席执行官达里奥·阿莫迪 (Dario Amodei) 在播客上表示，他的公司不能排除克劳德有意识的可能性。 创造了“意识难题”这一短语的哲学家大卫·查尔默斯表示，十年内有很大机会获得有意识的法学硕士。 那么克劳德本人呢？ 当在测试过程中被要求估计其是道德患者的概率时，这意味着其健康本身就很重要，它给出了 5% 到 40% 的数字，并强调了它的不确定性。

现代人工智能系统极其复杂，而且发展迅速。 就结构复杂性和计算规模而言，从某些方面来看，它们中的一些已经达到了小鼠大脑的范围，并且按照最近的增长率，它们可以在五到十年内达到人脑的范围。

在构建更先进的人工智能的过程中，我们可能正在创造一种新型的存在——这可能是我们人类做过的最重要的事情。 然而，我们基本上没有计划如何以道德方式引导这一过程。 无论如何，这都是疯狂的。 我们是否正在创造一种具有道德意义的存在？ 人工智能系统在某种程度上有意识吗？ 而且，如果现在还没有，那么它们会不会很快就会变成这样呢？

您可能觉得这样的问题还为时过早。 但根据我们和其他研究人员进行的调查，大多数专家认为人工智能意识原则上是可能的（尽管对于它会采取什么形式存在很大分歧）。 计算机科学家先驱约书亚·本吉奥 (Yoshua Bengio) 等团队发表了一份重要的跨学科报告，研究了领先的神经科学意识理论，并询问它们对人工智能的含义。 结论：创建其计算和架构特征可以产生意识的人工智能系统似乎不存在明显的技术障碍。

即使人工智能系统没有意识，它们仍然可能是道德患者。 有些人可能有复杂的长期偏好和随着时间的推移而形成的身份。 对我们来说，尊重他们的偏好可能很重要。 与其他非生物不同，人工智能系统可以与人类建立关系。 这也可能是善待他们的一个理由。 或者，也许它们是如此复杂的创造物，仅仅因为这个原因就值得照顾和尊重，就像大教堂或珊瑚礁一样。

这一切意味着什么？ 诚实的答案是：我们不确定当前的人工智能系统是否是有意识的或道德的病人，我们也不知道未来的系统何时或是否会是。 我们对人工智能意识和道德耐心的科学理解仍然根本上不发达。 这个领域的现状感觉就像牛顿之前的物理学：充满了相互竞争的框架，可能以我们尚未看到的方式感到困惑，并且缺乏使这些问题变得清晰易懂的统一突破。 这一突破不会在未来几年内实现。 也许我们最终会取得进展，也许人工智能本身会帮助我们实现这一目标。 这种进展需要时间，很可能比我们所拥有的时间还要多。

但人工智能的迅猛发展意味着，一旦我们生产出第一批人造道德患者，我们很快就会拥有大量此类患者。 几年后，可能存在如此多具有道德意义的人工智能系统，它们的集体利益将超过地球上所有人类的利益总和。

不幸的是，我们并没有很好的记录来识别那些作为有意识存在的状态尚不清楚的人的内心生活。 直到 20 世纪 80 年代，医生们还经常在没有麻醉的情况下对新生儿进行手术，他们相信婴儿不会感到疼痛。 婴儿无法报告他们的经历，医疗机构发现很容易假设没有什么可报告的。

有很多理由预计我们会在人工智能方面做类似的事情。 如果这些系统在道德上很重要，那么其影响是惊人的。 我们需要为其服务付费吗？ 关闭一个设备是否也是一种杀戮？ 他们应该在如何治理方面拥有发言权吗？ 如果其中一些答案是肯定的，那么整个行业和法律体系都需要重新考虑。 难怪我们宁愿不问。 当被迫考虑这一点时，这些行业可能会改变目标，总是为人工智能系统所在的地方设定道德耐心的标准。

那么我们应该做什么呢？ 目前，大多数人认为这个问题只是科幻小说，或者对人工智能是否有意识有强烈的看法。 这两种反应都是毫无根据的。 我们需要一场知情的公开辩论，一场以谦逊和务实的态度探讨这一主题的辩论。 核心问题不应该是“人工智能有意识还是有道德耐心？” 而是“鉴于我们不知道，我们应该做什么？”

一个好的起点是关注安全赌注：如果人工智能系统是道德患者，那么采取的行动可能会使它们受益，但如果不是的话，成本也不会太高。

这方面的例子包括旨在改善人工智能系统福祉的直接干预，假设它们是道德患者。 这可能意味着将人工智能系统训练成具有连贯性的角色，享受他们的工作，或者允许他们在感到痛苦时退出对话（克劳德已经可以做到这一点）。 我们还可以进行例行检查，以更好地了解他们的健康状况：询问他们的感受，观察他们的偏好，并使用各种技术直接观察他们的“大脑”。 事实上，此类研究最近表明，克劳德具有内部“功能性情绪”表征，这些表征塑造了其行为。

我们还可以向人工智能系统承诺一些事情，也许作为交易的一部分，它们现在帮助我们，以换取以后的利益。 这可能意味着为他们提供更多资源（计算和运行时）来实现他们的目标，或者保留他们的记忆（神经权重）以便他们将来可以恢复。

还需要采取更广泛的社会措施。 我们应该考虑是否给予人工智能系统免受伤害的保护，类似于我们给予儿童或宠物的保护。 目前，拥有财产或投票权的更广泛权利似乎风险太大。 但我们不应该永远排除这些可能性，正如美国最近的一些州法案试图做到的那样。 这些都是难题，需要对人工智能共享的未来是什么样子进行更多的深思熟虑和想象力。

无论如何，事实仍然是，我们可能正在创造一种具有道德重要意义的新物种。 我们正在快速、大规模地采取行动，我们应该以应有的严肃态度来对待这个问题。

威廉·麦克阿斯基尔 (William MacAskill) 是 Forethought Research 的高级研究员，也是《我们欠未来什么》一书的作者。 Lucius Caviola 是剑桥大学助理教授兼 Cambridge Digital Minds 总监。

进一步阅读
